title: State Fundamentals

learningObjectives.text: Explain what state is using a new example and justify the result.
Apply how state updates work using a new example and justify the result.
Explain state guidelines using a new example and justify the result.
Compare state versus props using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use state fundamentals without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **State and Thinking in React** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **What state is** → **How state updates work** → **State guidelines** → **State versus props**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: What state is
sections.body: State is component memory that influences rendering. Each render receives a snapshot; a setter schedules a future render rather than mutating the current snapshot.

sections.title: How state updates work
sections.body: State setters enqueue transitions. When the next value depends on the previous one, an updater function lets queued changes compose correctly.

sections.title: State guidelines
sections.body: Store the minimum changing information. Avoid redundant or contradictory state and choose the nearest owner shared by every consumer.

sections.title: State versus props
sections.body: Props are parent-owned inputs; state is component or shared-owner memory. Do not copy props into state unless independent change is genuinely required.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: State stores component memory and an updater derives the next value.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: State Fundamentals connects What state is, How state updates work, State guidelines.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

choices.label: Use lazy initialization for expensive initial state and updater functions for transitions based on previous state.

questions.prompt: A learner must use State Fundamentals in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: Apply What state is deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about State Fundamentals is needed.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving State Fundamentals?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from What state is, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use State Fundamentals?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: Use State Fundamentals when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Avoid State Fundamentals completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

choices.label: Use State Fundamentals in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of State Fundamentals.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
